Article is about Bar-le-Duc (Meuse), WD 48.77N,5.17E. Most printed points (48.08-50.17N, 3.67-5.5E) cluster near this within tolerance. A repeated distinct pair (50.1N,28.5E, in EB.4/10/11) is grossly different -- matches Bar in Podolia, Ukraine (~49.08N,27.68E), evidently a conflated second 'Bar' headword, not the article's subject.

WD Baracoa, Cuba = 20.34N,74.50W. EB.11 (and the article's own opening text, W.74.50 N.20.25) matches almost exactly. Majority editions (EB.10/4/5/9) print 76.17W/21.08N instead, ~1.7 deg off in longitude -- plausible period surveying variance between editions, not a gross error.

Identified as Baranyavár (now Branjin Vrh, Croatia), WD 45.72N,18.52E -- matches description (Lower Hungary, between Buda and Belgrade, taken from Turks 1684). EB.11 prints 18.08E (correct). EB.10/EB.9 print 10.08E and EB.4 prints 20.08E -- both digit-corrupted versions of 18 (majority of editions wrong).

Berkhamsted, Hertfordshire, WD 51.76N,0.56W. All 3 editions consistently print N.Lat 45.49 (45.82) -- longitude matches well but latitude is off by ~6 degrees, an original-text error (51 deg 45' apparently garbled to 45 deg 49', degree/minute figures transposed) propagated unchanged across editions rather than an OCR artifact.

Barnstaple, Devon, WD 51.08N,4.05W. Majority printed points (51.25N,4.08W) match closely. One distinct pair (53.58N,1.33W, EB.10/EB.5) is a different place in northern England (roughly the Barnsley/Yorkshire area), likely a conflated homonym unrelated to the described Devonshire port.
St Barthélemy, Caribbean, WD 17.90N,62.83W. Printed points (18.10-18.27N,62.25W) are all within ~0.6 deg -- consistent with period surveying precision.
Old name for Beirut, WD 33.89N,35.51E. Printed points (33.5N, 34.33-34.67E) are within ~1.2 deg -- reasonable period match.
Same identification as BASARTSCHICK (Pazardzhik, Bulgaria). Printed points (41.67-41.82N,24.5E) all within ~0.5 deg of WD 42.19N,24.33E.

Capital of Corsica, WD 42.70N,9.45E. The 'agree'-flagged cluster (42.58N,9.7E) matches closely. A second distinct cluster (39.67N,10.58E) is off by several degrees -- plausibly a conflated/erroneous second entry, not the primary match.

Capital of Corsica, WD 42.70N,9.45E. The 'agree'-flagged cluster (42.58N,9.7E) matches closely. A second distinct cluster (39.67N,10.58E) is off by several degrees -- plausibly a conflated/erroneous second entry, not the primary match.

Batticaloa, east coast of Ceylon/Sri Lanka, WD 7.72N,81.70E. EB.11 prints 81.05E (correct, matches well). EB.10/EB.4/EB.5/EB.9 (majority) all print 18.05E instead -- a digit transposition of 81 into 18, repeated across most editions.

Opening text explicitly says 'town of Bresse' with coords E.4.54 N.46.20, which matches Bâgé-le-Châtel, Ain (Bresse region), WD 46.31N,4.93E, almost exactly (this is the EB.4 alternate/digit-flagged point). However the majority of editions (EB.10/11/4-primary/9, 'agree') print 47.5N,0.17E instead, which matches a different, unrelated town: Baugé-en-Anjou, Maine-et-Loire (WD 47.54N,0.10W) -- an apparent cross-edition conflation of two similarly-named French towns.

Baiona, Galicia, Spain (near Tuy, as described), WD 42.12N,8.85W. The digit-flagged point (43.05N,9.5W) is within ~1 deg. A separate cluster (48.5N,14.7E, in most editions) is grossly different -- likely a conflated unrelated 'Bayon' entry (Central European location), not the described Galician town.

Episcopal city in Oise, France, WD 49.43N,2.09E. Majority printed cluster (49.43N,2.25E) matches closely. A second cluster (44.03N,1.72E) is a different place further south, likely a conflated homonym (e.g. Beauvais-sur-Tescou, Tarn), not the cathedral city described.

Bedale, North Riding of Yorkshire, WD 54.29N,1.59W. Majority printed cluster (54.5N,1.67W) matches closely. EB.4/EB.5 print a grossly wrong longitude (31.0W) -- an isolated digit-corruption misprint in those two editions only.

Białogard (Belgard), East Pomerania, WD 54.01N,15.99E. Printed 54.17N,16.08E is within ~0.2 deg across all 5 editions.

Belmonte Calabro (Q53835, WD 39.17N 16.08E); majority editions (39.33,16.83) within ~0.8deg. EB.11 alone misprints latitude as 30.3333 (a likely 9->0 digit misread of 39), off by 9deg.

Belz, Ukraine (Q156565, WD 50.38N 24.02E). Majority editions (50.08,25.25) within ~1.2deg. EB.11's raw text 'E. Long. 24.' (no minutes) matches almost exactly; the parsed lon=0.4 for that point is an extraction artifact (24 read as minutes, not degrees), not a printed error.

Article describes Bergues (St Winoc), France (Q244934, WD 50.97N 2.43E); best-matching editions (EB.11: 50.95,2.42; EB.4/5: 50.95,2.58) within ~0.15deg. Article describes Bergen auf Rügen, Pomerania, but every edition also carries a coordinate (60.18,5.75) matching Bergen, Norway (WD ~60.39,5.33) almost exactly - a different same-named place. 3 of 5 editions (10,4,5) additionally carry the article's own accurate value (54.5,13.0), which matches Bergen auf Rugen (WD Q492538, 54.42N 13.43E) within 0.5deg; text is explicitly swallowed into a following 'Bergen-op-Zoom' article.

Ancient Bergomum = Bergamo, Italy (Q628, WD 45.70N 9.67E). Raw text reads 'E. Long. 10.' (whole degrees), matching within ~0.3deg; the parsed lon=0.1667 is an extraction artifact (10 misread as minutes), not a printed misprint.
WD Bern = 46.95N 7.45E. Printed latitude 40.0 (vs actual ~46.95) is off by ~7deg while longitude (7.67) is accurate; mechanism likely digit misread, '46' printed/OCR'd as '40'.

Opening describes Bialogorod/Akkerman on the Black Sea (=Bilhorod-Dnistrovskyi, Q672990, WD 46.19N 30.34E), but all editions print (46.4,22.83), which matches Alba Iulia/Balgrad, Romania (Q174665, WD 46.08N 23.57E) within ~0.75deg instead - plausibly a period naming collision, since both 'Bialogorod' (Slavic) and old names for Alba Iulia translate to 'white town/castle'.

Bidache, France (Q197008, WD 43.48N 1.14W). Majority editions (41.52,-1.15) are off by ~1.97deg latitude with accurate longitude, plausibly an era surveying/reference error. EB.4/5 additionally show a wild longitude (-10.0), an extra digit-error layered on top of the same latitude value.

Article opening explicitly describes 'promontory of Peru... South sea' and gives coords matching the second printed pair (W81.10-81.17, N11.30-11.83). Zaltbommel, Gelderland, Netherlands (WD 51.812,5.247) vs printed 52.0,5.8333 (majority of eds) - diff lat 0.19, lon 0.59. EB.11 lon=3.8333 (diff 1.41) is a separate digit-flagged variant, still within tolerance.

WD Borgloon, Belgium: 50.802N,5.343E. Printed 50.83,5.47 across all 5 editions, within 0.15 deg.

Entry conflates several distinct towns named Borgo. Primary described place (Borgo/Porvoo, Finland, gulf of Finland) matches WD 60.39N,25.66E well via EB.10 point (60.57,26.42, ~0.8 deg off). Other points belong to Borgo San Sepolcro, Tuscany (43.5,13.0 vs actual ~43.57,12.14 - correct, own knowledge) and other Italian Borgos (44.58,10.6 / 44.83,11.0) not individually confirmed. EB.11's second Sweden/Finland point has a garbled longitude (0.43) that is a data-extraction artifact (raw text '26.' parsed as minutes), not a print error.

WD Baracoa, Cuba = 20.34N,74.50W. EB.11 (and the article's own opening text, W.74.50 N.20.25) matches almost exactly. Majority editions (EB.10/4/5/9) print 76.17W/21.08N instead, ~1.7 deg off in longitude -- plausible period surveying variance between editions, not a gross error.

Identified as Baranyavár (now Branjin Vrh, Croatia), WD 45.72N,18.52E -- matches description (Lower Hungary, between Buda and Belgrade, taken from Turks 1684). EB.11 prints 18.08E (correct). EB.10/EB.9 print 10.08E and EB.4 prints 20.08E -- both digit-corrupted versions of 18 (majority of editions wrong).

Barnstaple, Devon, WD 51.08N,4.05W. Majority printed points (51.25N,4.08W) match closely. One distinct pair (53.58N,1.33W, EB.10/EB.5) is a different place in northern England (roughly the Barnsley/Yorkshire area), likely a conflated homonym unrelated to the described Devonshire port.
St Barthélemy, Caribbean, WD 17.90N,62.83W. Printed points (18.10-18.27N,62.25W) are all within ~0.6 deg -- consistent with period surveying precision.
Old name for Beirut, WD 33.89N,35.51E. Printed points (33.5N, 34.33-34.67E) are within ~1.2 deg -- reasonable period match.
Pazardzhik, Bulgaria (on the Maritsa/Meritz river as described), WD 42.19N,24.33E. Printed 41.82N,24.5E is within ~0.4 deg.

Capital of Corsica, WD 42.70N,9.45E. The 'agree'-flagged cluster (42.58N,9.7E) matches closely. A second distinct cluster (39.67N,10.58E) is off by several degrees -- plausibly a conflated/erroneous second entry, not the primary match.

Batticaloa, east coast of Ceylon/Sri Lanka, WD 7.72N,81.70E. EB.11 prints 81.05E (correct, matches well). EB.10/EB.4/EB.5/EB.9 (majority) all print 18.05E instead -- a digit transposition of 81 into 18, repeated across most editions.

Opening text explicitly says 'town of Bresse' with coords E.4.54 N.46.20, which matches Bâgé-le-Châtel, Ain (Bresse region), WD 46.31N,4.93E, almost exactly (this is the EB.4 alternate/digit-flagged point). However the majority of editions (EB.10/11/4-primary/9, 'agree') print 47.5N,0.17E instead, which matches a different, unrelated town: Baugé-en-Anjou, Maine-et-Loire (WD 47.54N,0.10W) -- an apparent cross-edition conflation of two similarly-named French towns.

Beaumont, Hainaut, Belgium (ceded to French 1684, matches description), WD 50.24N,4.24E. Printed 49.15N,2.48E is off by ~1.1 deg lat / ~1.75 deg lon -- larger than a tight match but plausible as era surveying imprecision, consistent across all 5 editions (not a random digit error).

Episcopal city in Oise, France, WD 49.43N,2.09E. Majority printed cluster (49.43N,2.25E) matches closely. A second cluster (44.03N,1.72E) is a different place further south, likely a conflated homonym (e.g. Beauvais-sur-Tescou, Tarn), not the cathedral city described.

Opening says 'city of Burgundy' with coords E.4.50 N.47 -- matches Beaune (WD 47.02N,4.84E) almost exactly; this corresponds to the EB.11 digit-flagged point (47.0N,4.83E). However the majority of editions ('agree' flag) print 46.75N,1.08W instead, which matches a different swallowed headword visible in the same OCR block: Beauvoir-sur-Mer, Vendée (WD 46.92N,2.04W) -- most editions' printed coordinate is for that different coastal town, not Beaune.

Bedale, North Riding of Yorkshire, WD 54.29N,1.59W. Majority printed cluster (54.5N,1.67W) matches closely. EB.4/EB.5 print a grossly wrong longitude (31.0W) -- an isolated digit-corruption misprint in those two editions only.

Identified as Bidar, Deccan (under Mughal/'Great Mogul' rule after 1657, matching description), WD 17.90N,77.55E. EB.10/EB.11 print 16.83N,81.17E -- lat close, lon off by ~3.6 deg, plausible period surveying error for an interior Deccan town. EB.4/EB.5 print 95.17E instead, off by ~17.6 deg -- a further, worse corruption in those two editions.
Białogard (Belgard), East Pomerania, WD 54.01N,15.99E. Printed 54.17N,16.08E is within ~0.2 deg across all 5 editions.

Opening's own coordinate (E31.0 N46.30) matches Bilhorod-Dnistrovskyi/Akkerman (Q672990, WD 46.19N 30.34E) within ~0.7deg. Belz, Ukraine (Q156565, WD 50.38N 24.02E). Majority editions (50.08,25.25) within ~1.2deg. EB.11's raw text 'E. Long. 24.' (no minutes) matches almost exactly; the parsed lon=0.4 for that point is an extraction artifact (24 read as minutes, not degrees), not a printed error.

Opening describes Ramsey Abbey, Huntingdonshire (52.45,-0.11). Majority-agreed printed coords (51.92,-5.33) instead fit Ramsey Island, Pembrokeshire, Wales. EB.11 alone carries a second, more accurate point (52.43,-0.32).

True Ravenglass, Cumberland is ~3.4°W; printed consistently as E.Long ~0.08 across most editions -- an E/W hemisphere error (west longitude collapsed to near-zero/east). EB.5 additionally drops latitude to 51.33 (further corruption).
Article covers two towns (Reggio Calabria and Reggio Emilia); both printed coordinate pairs land within ~0.5° of their respective true positions across all editions.

Opening describes a castle on the Lower Rhine in Prussia (fits Rheinfels near St. Goar, ~50.14,7.70, matching the 'differs' point 50.05,7.72). The commonly 'agreed' point (47.67,7.88) across most editions instead is Rheinfelden, Switzerland -- a different place sharing the name.

EB.10's 42.0,12.13 is close to true Ronciglione. EB.5/9 raw 'E. Long. 13.' (bare degree, no minutes) was mis-parsed as 0.22 by the extraction pipeline; read literally as 13°E it is also close (true 12.2°E).

Rostov Velikiy on Lake Nero ('Coteri' likely a garbled rendering). Majority printed coords (57.08,40.42) are within ~1° of true position. EB.9 alone has latitude misprinted as 37 instead of 57 (digit 5\u21923).

Opening describes Salamis island near Athens (site of the Greek-Persian naval battle), true position ~37.97N,23.5E. Majority editions (EB.4,5,9) print longitude 18°27'E, ~11° east of true Saluzzo, Piedmont (7°29'E) -- looks like conflation with an unrelated Balkan location. EB.10/EB.11 preserve the accurate ~7°29'E.
Majority editions print 39.83,69.0, ~2° east of true Samarkand -- plausible era-level surveying error for interior Central Asia. EB.1's bare-degree reading (66°E,40°N) is closer to the true value.

Article is about Bar-le-Duc (Meuse), WD 48.77N,5.17E. Most printed points (48.08-50.17N, 3.67-5.5E) cluster near this within tolerance. A repeated distinct pair (50.1N,28.5E, in EB.4/10/11) is grossly different -- matches Bar in Podolia, Ukraine (~49.08N,27.68E), evidently a conflated second 'Bar' headword, not the article's subject.

WD Baracoa, Cuba = 20.34N,74.50W. EB.11 (and the article's own opening text, W.74.50 N.20.25) matches almost exactly. Majority editions (EB.10/4/5/9) print 76.17W/21.08N instead, ~1.7 deg off in longitude -- plausible period surveying variance between editions, not a gross error.

Identified as Baranyavár (now Branjin Vrh, Croatia), WD 45.72N,18.52E -- matches description (Lower Hungary, between Buda and Belgrade, taken from Turks 1684). EB.11 prints 18.08E (correct). EB.10/EB.9 print 10.08E and EB.4 prints 20.08E -- both digit-corrupted versions of 18 (majority of editions wrong).

Berkhamsted, Hertfordshire, WD 51.76N,0.56W. All 3 editions consistently print N.Lat 45.49 (45.82) -- longitude matches well but latitude is off by ~6 degrees, an original-text error (51 deg 45' apparently garbled to 45 deg 49', degree/minute figures transposed) propagated unchanged across editions rather than an OCR artifact.

Barnstaple, Devon, WD 51.08N,4.05W. Majority printed points (51.25N,4.08W) match closely. One distinct pair (53.58N,1.33W, EB.10/EB.5) is a different place in northern England (roughly the Barnsley/Yorkshire area), likely a conflated homonym unrelated to the described Devonshire port.
St Barthélemy, Caribbean, WD 17.90N,62.83W. Printed points (18.10-18.27N,62.25W) are all within ~0.6 deg -- consistent with period surveying precision.
Old name for Beirut, WD 33.89N,35.51E. Printed points (33.5N, 34.33-34.67E) are within ~1.2 deg -- reasonable period match.
Same identification as BASARTSCHICK (Pazardzhik, Bulgaria). Printed points (41.67-41.82N,24.5E) all within ~0.5 deg of WD 42.19N,24.33E.

Capital of Corsica, WD 42.70N,9.45E. The 'agree'-flagged cluster (42.58N,9.7E) matches closely. A second distinct cluster (39.67N,10.58E) is off by several degrees -- plausibly a conflated/erroneous second entry, not the primary match.

Capital of Corsica, WD 42.70N,9.45E. The 'agree'-flagged cluster (42.58N,9.7E) matches closely. A second distinct cluster (39.67N,10.58E) is off by several degrees -- plausibly a conflated/erroneous second entry, not the primary match.

Batticaloa, east coast of Ceylon/Sri Lanka, WD 7.72N,81.70E. EB.11 prints 81.05E (correct, matches well). EB.10/EB.4/EB.5/EB.9 (majority) all print 18.05E instead -- a digit transposition of 81 into 18, repeated across most editions.

Opening text explicitly says 'town of Bresse' with coords E.4.54 N.46.20, which matches Bâgé-le-Châtel, Ain (Bresse region), WD 46.31N,4.93E, almost exactly (this is the EB.4 alternate/digit-flagged point). However the majority of editions (EB.10/11/4-primary/9, 'agree') print 47.5N,0.17E instead, which matches a different, unrelated town: Baugé-en-Anjou, Maine-et-Loire (WD 47.54N,0.10W) -- an apparent cross-edition conflation of two similarly-named French towns.

Baiona, Galicia, Spain (near Tuy, as described), WD 42.12N,8.85W. The digit-flagged point (43.05N,9.5W) is within ~1 deg. A separate cluster (48.5N,14.7E, in most editions) is grossly different -- likely a conflated unrelated 'Bayon' entry (Central European location), not the described Galician town.

Agree pts (~42.83,18.17) match Dubrovnik (capital of Dalmatia, per opening) well; a second coordinate pair (37.0,14.98) present in several editions actually belongs to Ragusa, Sicily, a same-named but different town bundled in the same article.

Agree pts (~42.83,18.17) match Dubrovnik (capital of Dalmatia, per opening) well; a second coordinate pair (37.0,14.98) present in several editions actually belongs to Ragusa, Sicily, a same-named but different town bundled in the same article.

Opening describes Ramsey Abbey, Huntingdonshire (52.45,-0.11). Majority-agreed printed coords (51.92,-5.33) instead fit Ramsey Island, Pembrokeshire, Wales. EB.11 alone carries a second, more accurate point (52.43,-0.32).
Majority editions (10,4,5,9) print E.Long 22°24' (~4.2° too far east of true Racibórz 18°13'); EB.11 preserves the accurate 18°10'. EB.1's unrelated 25°N/80°E is wild garble, possibly contamination.

True Ravenglass, Cumberland is ~3.4°W; printed consistently as E.Long ~0.08 across most editions -- an E/W hemisphere error (west longitude collapsed to near-zero/east). EB.5 additionally drops latitude to 51.33 (further corruption).
Article covers two towns (Reggio Calabria and Reggio Emilia); both printed coordinate pairs land within ~0.5° of their respective true positions across all editions.

Opening describes a castle on the Lower Rhine in Prussia (fits Rheinfels near St. Goar, ~50.14,7.70, matching the 'differs' point 50.05,7.72). The commonly 'agreed' point (47.67,7.88) across most editions instead is Rheinfelden, Switzerland -- a different place sharing the name.

EB.10's 42.0,12.13 is close to true Ronciglione. EB.5/9 raw 'E. Long. 13.' (bare degree, no minutes) was mis-parsed as 0.22 by the extraction pipeline; read literally as 13°E it is also close (true 12.2°E).
Majority editions (10,11,9) print coords within ~0.4° of true Rosetta/Rashid, Egypt. EB.4 alone has a wild longitude outlier (41.58 vs 30.75), an isolated misprint.

Rostov Velikiy on Lake Nero ('Coteri' likely a garbled rendering). Majority printed coords (57.08,40.42) are within ~1° of true position. EB.9 alone has latitude misprinted as 37 instead of 57 (digit 5\u21923).

Opening describes Salamis island near Athens (site of the Greek-Persian naval battle), true position ~37.97N,23.5E. All editions consistently print ~37.53N,34.0E, which corresponds far better to ancient Salamis in Cyprus (~35.18N,33.90E) than to the Greek island -- longitude in particular (34.0 vs 23.5) is off by >10°.

Majority editions (10,11,9) print latitude as 24°10'N, ~19.5° south of true Salona (Dalmatia, near Split, ~43.5N); likely leading-digit corruption (44\u219224). EB.5 alone preserves the accurate ~44°10'N.
All editions (including EB.1's bare-degree 24°/41°) print coordinates within ~1° of true Thessaloniki.

Majority editions (EB.4,5,9) print longitude 18°27'E, ~11° east of true Saluzzo, Piedmont (7°29'E) -- looks like conflation with an unrelated Balkan location. EB.10/EB.11 preserve the accurate ~7°29'E.
Majority editions print 39.83,69.0, ~2° east of true Samarkand -- plausible era-level surveying error for interior Central Asia. EB.1's bare-degree reading (66°E,40°N) is closer to the true value.

Compound headword bundling several distinct 'Santa' places (Santa Cruz de la Sierra Bolivia, Santa Cruz/Agadir Morocco, a Pacific island, a Guiana location) all under one article, per opening's own 'Santa, Santa-Fe' aside. The point closest to the described Santa Cruz de la Sierra, Bolivia (-20.67,-59.58) is off by ~3° -- plausible era-level error.

Majority editions (EB.4,5,9) print latitude 39.17, ~2.8° north of true Santorini -- era-plausible error. EB.10/11 (36.63,25.6) are much closer to the true position.
Majority editions (10,11,9) place Savannah, Georgia within ~1° of its true position. EB.4/5 alone show a gross longitude error (-101.3 vs -81.1, ~20° off), an isolated misprint.

All editions consistently print 'E.Long 54°18\', N.Lat 0°3\'' -- the latitude and longitude magnitudes are transposed (true Scarborough is Lat 54°17'N, Long 0°24'W); an equatorial latitude for a Yorkshire coastal town is implausible on its face.
All editions print coordinates within ~0.5° of true \u015awidnica, Poland.
Majority editions (11,4,5,9) are within ~0.3° of true Bad Segeberg. EB.10 alone shows a longitude misprinted as 20°9' instead of 10°9' (leading digit doubled), an isolated error.
All editions print coordinates within ~0.5° of true Senj, Croatia, despite internal 'contra' cross-edition flags.

Primary Segovia, Spain point (41.0,-3.8) is close to true position. Article also covers Nueva Segovia (Nicaragua), Segovia (Venezuela) and Segovia (Philippines), whose printed points are each individually within period/correct tolerance of their respective true locations.

All editions print coordinates within ~0.9° of true Smederevo, Serbia.
Majority editions are within ~0.2° of true Saint-Louis, Senegal. EB.16 alone is a substantial outlier (14.17,-10.5), an isolated error.
Majority editions within ~0.7° of true Smolensk. EB.4 alone has a moderate longitude/latitude digit discrepancy, an isolated deviation.
All editions print coordinates within ~1.2° of true Split, Croatia.
Majority editions (10,5,9) print latitude 30°0'N, ~8.8° south of true Stromboli -- likely leading-digit corruption (38\u219230). EB.11 alone preserves the accurate ~38°50'N.
All editions, including EB.1's bare-degree reading (8°E literal), are within ~0.8° of true Tabarka, Tunisia.

Majority editions (10,11,5,9) place latitude ~38.8, ~3° north of true Tangier -- consistent across many editions, suggesting a genuine era-level error rather than a random misprint. EB.16 and EB.1 (bare-degree corrected) are much closer to the true position.
Majority editions within ~0.3° of true Taranto. EB.9 alone has latitude misprinted as 45 instead of 40, an isolated digit error.
All editions consistently print latitude ~28°45'N, ~10° south of true Terceira, Azores (38°45'N) -- likely leading-digit corruption (38\u219228), repeated uniformly across every edition.
Majority editions print 129°E, ~1.6° east of true Ternate -- borderline but plausible given historically poor longitude measurement in the East Indies before reliable chronometers.
Majority editions within ~0.15° of true T\u00e9touan, Morocco. EB.16 alone has a gross longitude misprint (50°18' instead of ~5°18', an extra inserted digit).
True Toledo, Spain is ~4.03°W; majority editions print it as ~3.25°E -- an E/W hemisphere flip (or meridian confusion), a >7° swing.
Majority editions (EB.4,5,9) print longitude ~-72.9, ~2.7° east of true Tol\u00fa, Colombia -- era-plausible. EB.10/11 (-75.42) are much closer.
All editions print coordinates within ~0.15° of true Trento, Italy.
Majority editions within ~0.1° of true Tonbridge, Kent. EB.4 alone has latitude misprinted ~3° too far north, an isolated error.
Majority editions within ~0.7° of true Tunis. EB.11 alone has longitude misprinted as 16°10' instead of 10°16' (digit substitution), an isolated error.
True Set\u00fabal ('St. Ubes'), Portugal is ~8.89°W; consistently printed across all editions as ~8.9-9.5°E -- an E/W hemisphere flip.
All editions print coordinates within ~1.2° of true Cabo de la Vela, Colombia.

Most editions (10,9) print longitude ~-37.4, ~59° off true Veracruz, Mexico (-96.13) -- far beyond any plausible era error, likely severe digit corruption; a real Vera Cruz does not exist at that Atlantic location. EB.11 alone is accurate (-96.25). EB.4 shows a separate large error (-102.25).
Majority editions within ~0.2° of true Vienna. EB.10 alone has longitude misprinted as 10°20' instead of 16°20' (digit substitution), an isolated error.
All editions, including EB.1's bare-degree reading, are within ~0.3° of true Ulm, Germany.
Majority editions within ~0.1° of true Vard\u00f8, Norway. EB.4 alone shows a moderate ~3° longitude deviation, era-plausible for that single edition.

Majority editions (10,5,9) print latitude as 50°14', exactly 2° south of true Warsaw -- looks like a simple digit substitution (52\u219250) but the magnitude falls in the era-plausible band. EB.11/EB.16 preserve the accurate 52°14'.
All editions, including EB.1's bare-degree reading, are within ~0.3° of true Waterford, Ireland.

Majority editions within ~0.3° of true Williamsburg, Virginia. EB.4 alone shows coordinates matching Bengal, India (22.45,86.0) -- apparent cross-contamination from the adjacent 'William's Fort' Bengal entry mentioned in the same passage.
All editions print coordinates within ~0.3° of true Wo\u0142\u00f3w, Poland.
Alternate spelling of Wolfsberg, Austria; printed coordinates within ~0.5° of true position across all editions.
Majority editions within ~0.1° of true Woolwich, London. EB.5 alone shows a gross error (52.17,-1.92), consistent with confusion with a Midlands town, an isolated misprint.
Majority editions within ~0.15° of true Worms, Germany. EB.4 alone has latitude misprinted ~9° too far south, an isolated error.

Opening describes the Swiss town near Constance/St Gallen (likely Wil); the matching point (47.57,9.07) is close to true position. A second point (51.17,1.07) under the same headword corresponds to the separate, real village of Wye, Kent, England.
